From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Layer of cells that line the inner surface of blood vessels

This article is about the lining of blood and lymphatic vessels. For the endothelium of the cornea, see [corneal endothelium](/wiki/Corneal_endothelium "Corneal endothelium").

The **endothelium** (pl.: **endothelia**) is a single layer of [squamous](/wiki/Squamous_epithelial_cell "Squamous epithelial cell") **endothelial cells** that line the interior surface of [blood vessels](/wiki/Blood_vessel "Blood vessel") and [lymphatic vessels](/wiki/Lymphatic_vessel "Lymphatic vessel").[[1]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-1) The endothelium forms an interface between circulating [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood") or [lymph](/wiki/Lymph "Lymph") in the [lumen](/wiki/Lumen_(anatomy) "Lumen (anatomy)") and the rest of the vessel wall.

Endothelial cells in direct contact with blood are called vascular endothelial cells whereas those in direct contact with [lymph](/wiki/Lymph "Lymph") are known as lymphatic endothelial cells. Vascular endothelial cells line the entire [circulatory system](/wiki/Circulatory_system "Circulatory system"), from the [heart](/wiki/Heart "Heart") to the smallest [capillaries](/wiki/Capillaries "Capillaries").

These cells have unique functions that include [fluid filtration](/wiki/Ultrafiltration "Ultrafiltration"), such as in the [glomerulus](/wiki/Glomerulus_(kidney) "Glomerulus (kidney)") of the kidney, [blood vessel tone](/wiki/Muscle_tone "Muscle tone"), [hemostasis](/wiki/Hemostasis "Hemostasis"), [neutrophil](/wiki/Neutrophil "Neutrophil") recruitment, and hormone trafficking. Endothelium of the interior surfaces of the heart chambers is called [endocardium](/wiki/Endocardium "Endocardium"). An impaired function can lead to serious health issues throughout the body.

## Structure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Structure")]

The endothelium is a thin layer of single flat ([squamous](/wiki/Squamous_epithelial_cell "Squamous epithelial cell")) cells that line the interior surface of [blood vessels](/wiki/Blood_vessel "Blood vessel") and [lymphatic vessels](/wiki/Lymphatic_vessel "Lymphatic vessel").[[1]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-1)

Endothelium is of [mesodermal](/wiki/Mesoderm "Mesoderm") origin. Both blood and lymphatic [capillaries](/wiki/Capillaries "Capillaries") are composed of a single layer of endothelial cells called a monolayer. In straight sections of a blood vessel, vascular endothelial cells typically align and elongate in the direction of fluid flow.[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

### Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Terminology")]

The [foundational model of anatomy](/wiki/Foundational_Model_of_Anatomy "Foundational Model of Anatomy"), an index of terms used to describe anatomical structures, makes a distinction between endothelial cells and [epithelial cells](/wiki/Epithelial_cells "Epithelial cells") on the basis of which tissues they develop from, and states that the presence of [vimentin](/wiki/Vimentin "Vimentin") rather than [keratin](/wiki/Keratin "Keratin") filaments separates these from epithelial cells.[[4]](#cite_note-urlFMA-4) Many considered the endothelium a specialized [epithelial](/wiki/Epithelial "Epithelial") tissue.[[5]](#cite_note-5) However, much research uncovered not only similarities but also differences between endothelia and epithelia.[[6]](#cite_note-6) The comparison is further complicated by the diversity that endothelial cells exhibit.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

## Function

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Function")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/2104_Three_Major_Capillary_Types.jpg/250px-2104_Three_Major_Capillary_Types.jpg)](/wiki/File:2104_Three_Major_Capillary_Types.jpg)

Endothelium lines the inner wall of vessels, shown here.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3d/Endocardium_and_subendocardium_histology.png/250px-Endocardium_and_subendocardium_histology.png)](/wiki/File:Endocardium_and_subendocardium_histology.png)

[Microscopic view](/wiki/Histology "Histology") showing endothelium (at top) inside the [heart](/wiki/Heart "Heart").

The endothelium forms an interface between circulating [blood](/wiki/Blood "Blood") or [lymph](/wiki/Lymph "Lymph") in the [lumen](/wiki/Lumen_(anatomy) "Lumen (anatomy)") and the rest of the vessel wall. This forms a barrier between vessels and tissues and controls the flow of substances and fluid into and out of a tissue. This controls the passage of materials and the transit of [white blood cells](/wiki/White_blood_cell "White blood cell") into and out of the bloodstream. Excessive or prolonged increases in permeability of the endothelium, as in cases of chronic inflammation, may lead to tissue swelling ([edema](/wiki/Edema "Edema")). Altered barrier function is also implicated in cancer extravasation.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

Endothelial cells are involved in many other aspects of vessel function, including:

* [Blood clotting](/wiki/Coagulation "Coagulation") ([thrombosis](/wiki/Thrombosis "Thrombosis") and [fibrinolysis](/wiki/Fibrinolysis "Fibrinolysis")). Under normal conditions, the endothelium provides a surface on which blood does not clot, because it contains and expresses substances that prevent clotting,[[9]](#cite_note-9) including [heparan sulfate](/wiki/Heparan_sulfate "Heparan sulfate") which acts as a [cofactor](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)") for activating [antithrombin](/wiki/Antithrombin "Antithrombin"), a protein that inactivates several factors in the coagulation cascade.[[10]](#cite_note-10)
* [Inflammation](/wiki/Inflammation "Inflammation").[[11]](#cite_note-li-2016-11) Endothelial cells actively signal to white blood cells of the immune system[[12]](#cite_note-12) during inflammation
* Formation of new blood vessels ([angiogenesis](/wiki/Angiogenesis "Angiogenesis")).
* Constriction and enlargement of the blood vessel, called [vasoconstriction](/wiki/Vasoconstriction "Vasoconstriction") and [vasodilation](/wiki/Vasodilation "Vasodilation"), and hence the control of [blood pressure](/wiki/Blood_pressure "Blood pressure")

### Blood vessel formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Blood vessel formation")]

The endothelium is involved in the formation of new blood vessels, called [angiogenesis](/wiki/Angiogenesis "Angiogenesis").[[13]](#cite_note-13) Angiogenesis is a crucial process for development of organs in the embryo and fetus,[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14) as well as repair of damaged areas.[[15]](#cite_note-15) The process is triggered by decreased tissue oxygen (hypoxia) or insufficient oxygen tension leading to the new development of blood vessels lined with endothelial cells. Angiogenesis is regulated by signals that promote and decrease the process. These pro- and antiangiogenic signals including integrins, chemokines, angiopoietins, oxygen sensing agents, junctional molecules and endogenous inhibitors.[[14]](#cite_note-:1-14) [Angiopoietin-2](/wiki/Angiopoietin-2 "Angiopoietin-2") works with VEGF to facilitate cell proliferation and migration of endothelial cells.

The general outline of angiogenesis is

* activating signals binding to surface receptors of vascular endothelial cells.
* activated endothelial cells release proteases leading to the degradation of the basement membrane
* endothelial cells are freed to migrate from the existing blood vessels and begin to proliferate to form extensions towards the source of the angiogenic stimulus.

### Host immune response

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Host immune response")]

Endothelial cells express a variety of immune genes in an organ-specific manner.[[16]](#cite_note-Krausgruber_2020-16) These genes include critical [immune mediators](/wiki/Cytokine "Cytokine") and proteins that facilitate [cellular communication](/wiki/Cell_signaling "Cell signaling") with hematopoietic immune cells.[[17]](#cite_note-17) Endothelial cells encode important features of the structural cell immune response in the [epigenome](/wiki/Epigenome "Epigenome") and can therefore respond swiftly to immunological challenges. The contribution to host immunity by non-hematopoietic cells, such as endothelium, is called "structural immunity".[[18]](#cite_note-Minton_2020-18)

## Clinical significance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Clinical significance")]

Main article: [Endothelial dysfunction](/wiki/Endothelial_dysfunction "Endothelial dysfunction")

[Endothelial dysfunction](/wiki/Endothelial_dysfunction "Endothelial dysfunction"), or the loss of proper endothelial function, is a hallmark for vascular diseases, and is often regarded as a key early event in the development of [atherosclerosis](/wiki/Atherosclerosis "Atherosclerosis").[[19]](#cite_note-botts-2021-19) Impaired endothelial function, causing hypertension and thrombosis, is often seen in patients with [coronary artery disease](/wiki/Coronary_artery_disease "Coronary artery disease"), [diabetes mellitus](/wiki/Diabetes_mellitus "Diabetes mellitus"), [hypertension](/wiki/Hypertension "Hypertension"), [hypercholesterolemia](/wiki/Hypercholesterolemia "Hypercholesterolemia"), as well as in [smokers](/wiki/Tobacco_smoking "Tobacco smoking"). Endothelial dysfunction has also been shown to be predictive of future adverse cardiovascular events including stroke, heart disease, and is also present in inflammatory disease such as rheumatoid arthritis, diabetes, and systemic lupus erythematosus.[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

Endothelial dysfunction is a result of changes in endothelial function.[[22]](#cite_note-22)[[23]](#cite_note-23) After fat ([lipid](/wiki/Lipid "Lipid")) accumulation and when stimulated by inflammation, endothelial cells become activated, which is characterized by the expression of molecules such as E-selectin, VCAM-1 and ICAM-1, which stimulate the adhesion of immune cells.[[24]](#cite_note-24) Additionally, [transcription factors](/wiki/Transcription_factor "Transcription factor"), which are substances which act to increase the production of proteins within cells, become activated; specifically AP-1 and [NF-κB](/wiki/NF-%CE%BAB "NF-κB"), leading to increased expression of cytokines such as [IL-1](/wiki/Interleukin-1_family "Interleukin-1 family"), [TNFα](/wiki/Tumor_necrosis_factor_alpha "Tumor necrosis factor alpha") and [IFNγ](/wiki/Interferon_gamma "Interferon gamma"), which promotes inflammation.[[25]](#cite_note-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26) This state of endothelial cells promotes accumulation of lipids and lipoproteins in the intima, leading to atherosclerosis, and the subsequent recruitment of white blood cells and platelets, as well as proliferation of smooth muscle cells, leading to the formation of a fatty streak. The lesions formed in the intima, and persistent inflammation lead to desquamation of endothelium, which disrupts the endothelial barrier, leading to injury and consequent dysfunction.[[27]](#cite_note-27) In contrast, inflammatory stimuli also activate NF-κB-induced expression of the deubiquitinase A20 ([TNFAIP3](/wiki/TNFAIP3 "TNFAIP3")), which has been shown to intrinsically repair the endothelial barrier.[[28]](#cite_note-Soni_2018-28)

One of the main mechanisms of endothelial dysfunction is the diminishing of [nitric oxide](/wiki/Nitric_oxide "Nitric oxide"), often due to high levels of [asymmetric dimethylarginine](/wiki/Asymmetric_dimethylarginine "Asymmetric dimethylarginine"), which interfere with the normal [L-arginine](/wiki/Arginine "Arginine")-stimulated [nitric oxide synthesis](/wiki/Nitric_oxide_synthase "Nitric oxide synthase") and so leads to hypertension. The most prevailing mechanism of endothelial dysfunction is an increase in [reactive oxygen species](/wiki/Reactive_oxygen_species "Reactive oxygen species"), which can impair nitric oxide production and activity via several mechanisms.[[29]](#cite_note-pmid15643116-29) The signalling protein [ERK5](/wiki/ERK5 "ERK5") is essential for maintaining normal endothelial cell function.[[30]](#cite_note-pmid19909257-30) A further consequence of damage to the endothelium is the release of pathological quantities of [von Willebrand factor](/wiki/Von_Willebrand_factor "Von Willebrand factor"), which promote platelet aggregation and adhesion to the subendothelium, and thus the formation of potentially fatal thrombi.

[Angiosarcoma](/wiki/Angiosarcoma "Angiosarcoma") is cancer of the endothelium and is rare with only 300 cases per year in the US.[[31]](#cite_note-31) However it generally has poor prognosis with a five-year survival rate of 35%.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

### Research

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Research")]

#### Cancer

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Cancer")]

It has been recognised that endothelial cells building tumour vasculature have distinct morphological characteristics, different origin compared to physiological endothelium, and distinct molecular signature, which gives an opportunity for implementation of new biomarkers of tumour angiogenesis and could provide new anti-angiogenic druggable targets.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

#### Diet

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Diet")]

A [healthy diet](/wiki/Healthy_diet "Healthy diet") abundant in fruits and vegetables has a beneficial impact on endothelial function, whilst a diet high in [red](/wiki/Red_meat "Red meat") and [processed meats](/wiki/Processed_meat "Processed meat"), fried foods, [refined grains](/wiki/Refined_grains "Refined grains") and [processed sugar](/wiki/Processed_sugar "Processed sugar") increases adhesion endothelial cells and atherogenic promoters.[[34]](#cite_note-34) High-fat diets adversely affect the endothelial function.[[35]](#cite_note-35)

A [Mediterranean diet](/wiki/Mediterranean_diet "Mediterranean diet") has been found to improve endothelial function in adults which can reduce risk of cardiovascular disease.[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) [Walnut](/wiki/Walnut "Walnut") consumption improves endothelial function.[[38]](#cite_note-38)[[39]](#cite_note-39)

#### Covid-19

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Covid-19")]

In April 2020, the presence of viral elements in endothelial cells of 3 patients who had died of [COVID-19](/wiki/Coronavirus_disease_2019 "Coronavirus disease 2019") was reported for the first time. The researchers from the [University of Zurich](/wiki/University_of_Zurich "University of Zurich") and [Harvard Medical School](/wiki/Harvard_Medical_School "Harvard Medical School") considered these findings to be a sign of a general [endotheliitis](/wiki/Endotheliitis "Endotheliitis") in different organs, an inflammatory response of the endothelium to the infection that can lead or at least contribute to multi-organ failure in Covid-19 patients with comorbidities such as diabetes mellitus, hypertension and cardiovascular disease.[[40]](#cite_note-pmid32325026-40)[[41]](#cite_note-41)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Endothelium&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: History")]

In 1865, the [Swiss](/wiki/Swiss_people "Swiss people") anatomist [Wilhelm His](/wiki/Wilhelm_His_Sr. "Wilhelm His Sr.") Sr. first coined the term "endothelium".[[42]](#cite_note-42) In 1958, A. S. Todd of the [University of St Andrews](/wiki/University_of_St_Andrews "University of St Andrews") demonstrated that endothelium in human blood vessels have fibrinolytic activity.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)
